Q1: I kept a pretty consistent routine throughout the course, honestly. I'd block out time in the mornings, usually around 9 AM, and try to get through my study sessions before lunch. Being a visual learner, I'd spend a lot of time watching the lecture videos and making color-coded notes. I aimed for about 32 hours a week, spread across most days, which felt manageable even if my motivation wasn't always there.
Q2: I definitely stuck to regular times each week because that's just how I function best. Sundays I'd plan out my week and schedule specific slots for each course. Deadlines would sometimes make me add extra hours, but the core routine stayed the same. I found that having that structure helped me get through the work even when I wasn't feeling particularly motivated.
Q3: Usually I logged in because it was just part of my daily schedule—like checking email, you know? I'd also log in when I knew new content was posted or when I needed to check on upcoming deadlines. Sometimes the notification emails would remind me to check in. It became almost automatic after a while, even if I wasn't always super engaged with what I was doing there.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks it just became habit. I'd wake up, have coffee, and log into the LMS before doing anything else. I think what made it stick was doing it at the same time every day—my brain just expected it. The high login frequency wasn't because I was super enthusiastic; it was more like brushing my teeth, just something I did without thinking much about it.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best visually. I'd watch them carefully and pause to take notes with diagrams and highlights. The visual presentations and any infographics in the course materials really helped concepts click for me. I also appreciated when instructors used slides with clear graphics rather than just walls of text.
Q6: I'll be honest, I mostly ignored the discussion forums beyond the bare minimum posts required. They felt time-consuming and I wasn't sure they'd help my grade much. Some of the supplementary readings I'd skim or skip entirely if they seemed too text-heavy. My task completion wasn't great because I prioritized what I thought would directly impact my exam score.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exam or assignments. Visual content that explained concepts clearly was always worth it to me. If something felt like busywork or wasn't going to be assessed, I'd question whether I should spend energy on it, especially when my motivation was already low.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't really afford mentally. With 15 courses to juggle and not much internal motivation pushing me, I had to pick my battles. Required stuff got done because it had to, but optional things felt like they were for students who had more energy or enthusiasm than I did at the time.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that wouldn't show up in the LMS. I'd download the lecture slides and videos, then review them on my tablet while lying in bed or at a coffee shop. I also made physical flashcards with diagrams and spent time just staring at my handwritten notes. That offline visual review was actually where a lot of my learning happened.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying from printed notes or reviewing diagrams I'd drawn, and none of that would register anywhere. I'd spend hours reorganizing my visual notes or creating mind maps on paper. Sometimes I'd rewatch downloaded videos multiple times, but since I wasn't streaming them live, the system wouldn't know I was engaged with the material.
Q11: Yeah, that was pretty much my standard approach. I'd access a video once to download it, then watch it three or four more times offline. Same with PDFs—I'd download them, print the important ones, and highlight them physically. The LMS would only show that initial access, not all the subsequent studying I did with those materials.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. The stress was building up and my motivation, which was already low, basically bottomed out. I kept going through the motions of my routine, but I wasn't really absorbing much. The mindfulness exercises they introduced helped a bit with the stress, but engagement still felt like pushing through mud.
Q13: The workload across all my courses definitely affected how I used the LMS. When I was overwhelmed, I'd still log in out of habit but just skim through things without really processing them. Fatigue made it hard to focus on the videos even though they're my preferred way to learn. I'd find myself watching but not really watching, if that makes sense.
Q14: Honestly, quite often. I'd have the video playing and technically be logged in, but my mind would be elsewhere worrying about other deadlines or just zoning out. My watch time looks decent at over 90 minutes, but some of that was definitely passive viewing where I wasn't really taking anything in. The routine was there, but the meaningful engagement wasn't always.
Q15: I postponed the bigger assignments more than I should have, which is probably why my completion rate is so low. Even knowing they were important, I'd tell myself I'd do them after watching more videos or reviewing more notes. I think the low motivation made starting feel overwhelming, so I'd stick to the comfortable routine of passive watching instead.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main thing that got me to actually submit work. My routine kept me logging in and watching content, but without a deadline looming, I wouldn't push myself to produce anything. When deadlines hit, I'd cram and rush through assignments, which probably hurt my scores. The structure helped but also stressed me out.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got decent scores, it validated my routine and kept me going. But when feedback was critical, it sometimes made me want to avoid that type of work rather than improve. My stress levels were already high, so negative feedback felt heavier than it probably should have. I appreciated feedback but didn't always act on it constructively.
Q18: The weekly structure of the course worked well with my routine-based approach. Having content released on a schedule meant I could plan when to watch and review. But the structure also meant that if I fell behind on assignments, it was hard to catch up while keeping up with new material. I stayed consistent with attendance but struggled with the output side.
Q19: The visual elements of the course—videos, diagrams, slides—made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was when there was too much reading or when discussion participation felt forced. Also, having so many small tasks made it overwhelming to track everything, and I'd end up just doing the viewing part and letting other tasks slide.
Q20: I think I'm actually an example of that pattern. I logged in frequently and watched a lot of videos, but my assignment completion was pretty low. For me, it was about the gap between passive consumption and active production. Watching felt manageable and fit my routine; creating assignments required motivation and energy I often didn't have. The system shows activity but not the struggle behind it.
Q21: I can see how that happens, especially for students who study more effectively offline. If someone downloads everything and studies from printed materials or their own notes, they might ace the exam without much LMS activity. I was kind of in between—high activity but mediocre performance because my activity was more routine than productive.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in, what you click on, and how long you spend on videos. That's useful for seeing routine and consistency. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether you're actually thinking or just going through motions. They also miss all the offline work, the stress you're feeling, and whether you're genuinely learning or just complying with expectations.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just showing up. It's when you're curious and thinking critically, not just checking boxes. I'll admit my engagement was more about compliance than genuine interest a lot of the time. True engagement should feel energizing, but for me it often felt like obligation.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was just going through the motions due to low motivation. It wouldn't capture the stress I was managing or how the mindfulness intervention helped me cope even if it didn't boost my grades. It also wouldn't show all my offline visual studying or that despite feeling happy on the surface, I was struggling internally to stay motivated and keep up with everything.
